questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
x ← 5
y ← 3
x ← x + y
DISPLAY(x)

questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: 3 is the value of y. The third line reassigns x to x + y = 5 + 3 = 8, so DISPLAY(x) outputs 8.
5 is the original value of x before reassignment. The third line sets x ← x + y = 5 + 3 = 8.
x starts at 5, y is 3. x ← x + y evaluates to 5 + 3 = 8, which is then stored in x. DISPLAY(x) outputs 8.
15 would result from multiplying (5 × 3), not adding. The operator used is +, giving 5 + 3 = 8.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: 0 is the initial value before the loop runs. The loop executes 4 times, incrementing count each time, so the final value is 4.
The loop runs 4 times, not 1 time. After all iterations, count equals 0 + 1 + 1 + 1 + 1 = 4.
The loop iterates exactly 4 times (not 3). count starts at 0 and is incremented 4 times: 0 → 1 → 2 → 3 → 4.
count starts at 0 and the loop runs exactly 4 times, adding 1 each iteration: 0 → 1 → 2 → 3 → 4. DISPLAY(count) outputs 4.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
numList ← [10, 20, 30, 40, 50]
DISPLAY(numList[3])

questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: 20 is at index 2. In CB pseudocode, lists use 1-based indexing — numList[3] refers to the third element, which is 30.
In CB pseudocode, list indexing is 1-based. numList[3] accesses the third element: 10 (index 1), 20 (index 2), 30 (index 3).
40 is at index 4. In 1-based indexing, numList[3] is the third element: 30.
50 is at index 5, the last element. numList[3] accesses the third element, which is 30.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.text: A
choices.explanation: "A" is displayed when score ≥ 90. Since score is 75, the condition 75 ≥ 90 is false, so the ELSE branch executes and displays "B".

choices.text: B
choices.explanation: score is 75. The condition 75 ≥ 90 evaluates to false, so the ELSE branch executes and DISPLAY("B") outputs "B".

choices.text: AB
choices.explanation: Only one branch of an IF/ELSE executes — not both. Since 75 < 90, only the ELSE branch runs, displaying "B".

choices.text: Nothing is displayed
choices.explanation: An IF/ELSE always executes one branch. Since the condition is false, the ELSE branch runs and displays "B".

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: 7 is the argument passed to square. The procedure returns n * n = 7 * 7 = 49, which is stored in result and displayed.
14 = 7 × 2. The procedure computes n * n = 7 * 7 = 49, not n * 2.
square(7) calls the procedure with n = 7. It returns 7 * 7 = 49. This value is stored in result, and DISPLAY(result) outputs 49.

choices.text: The code will not run because RETURN is not valid in CB pseudocode
choices.explanation: RETURN is a valid CB pseudocode statement used inside procedures to send a value back to the caller. The code runs and displays 49.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
myList ← [5, 10, 15]
APPEND(myList, 20)
DISPLAY(LENGTH(myList))

questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: 3 is the original length before APPEND. After APPEND(myList, 20), the list has 4 elements: [5, 10, 15, 20].
myList starts with 3 elements. APPEND(myList, 20) adds 20 to the end, making it [5, 10, 15, 20] with LENGTH 4.
20 is the value appended to the list. LENGTH(myList) returns the number of elements (4), not the last appended value.
50 would be the sum of the elements. LENGTH returns the count of elements, which is 4 after appending 20.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes procedural abstraction?

choices.text: Naming a list variable so that all elements can be accessed under a single name
choices.explanation: Naming a list to group elements under one variable is data abstraction, not procedural abstraction. Procedural abstraction involves grouping code into named procedures.

choices.text: Grouping code into a named procedure so it can be called without knowing its internal implementation
choices.explanation: Procedural abstraction allows a programmer to call a procedure by name without understanding how it works internally — the implementation details are hidden behind the procedure's interface.

choices.text: Replacing multiple variables with a single binary number
choices.explanation: This describes encoding, not abstraction. Procedural abstraction specifically refers to hiding implementation details within named procedures.

choices.text: Removing all comments from code to make it shorter
choices.explanation: Removing comments affects documentation, not abstraction. Procedural abstraction involves defining and using procedures to manage complexity.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the difference between linear search and binary search?

choices.text: Linear search requires a sorted list, while binary search works on any list
choices.explanation: This reverses the requirements. Binary search requires a sorted list; linear search works on both sorted and unsorted lists.

choices.text: Linear search checks every element one by one; binary search divides the list in half each step and requires the list to be sorted
choices.explanation: Linear search examines each element sequentially. Binary search compares the target to the middle element, halving the search space each step — but this only works if the list is sorted.

choices.text: Linear search is faster than binary search for large sorted lists
choices.explanation: Binary search is significantly faster on large sorted lists. Linear search is O(n) — it may check every element. Binary search is O(log n) — it halves the search space each step.

choices.text: Binary search and linear search always take the same number of steps on any list
choices.explanation: Binary search takes far fewer steps on large sorted lists. On a sorted list of 1,000 elements, binary search takes at most ~10 steps, while linear search may need all 1,000.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
a ← 10
b ← 20
temp ← a
a ← b
b ← temp
DISPLAY(a)
DISPLAY(b)

questions.question: What values are displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.text: 10 then 20
choices.explanation: This would be the original values before swapping. The code uses a temporary variable to swap a and b, so the outputs are 20 then 10.

choices.text: 20 then 10
choices.explanation: temp stores a's original value (10). Then a ← b makes a = 20. Then b ← temp makes b = 10. So DISPLAY(a) = 20, DISPLAY(b) = 10.

choices.text: 10 then 10
choices.explanation: Without the temp variable, assigning a ← b would lose a's original value. But temp preserves it — b gets 10 (from temp), and a gets 20. The result is 20 then 10.

choices.text: 20 then 20
choices.explanation: temp correctly preserves the original value of a. After the swap, a = 20 and b = 10 (not 20).

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
sum ← 0
scores ← [80, 90, 70, 100, 85]
FOR EACH s IN scores
{
  sum ← sum + s
}
DISPLAY(sum)

questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: 85 is the last element in the list. The loop accumulates the sum of all elements: 80 + 90 + 70 + 100 + 85 = 425.
The sum is an integer: 80 + 90 + 70 + 100 + 85 = 425, not an average or decimal value.
The FOR EACH loop iterates over each score in scores and adds it to sum. 80 + 90 + 70 + 100 + 85 = 425.
500 is not the correct sum. 80 + 90 + 70 + 100 + 85 = 425.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes a library in the context of programming?

choices.text: A structured list used to store multiple values under one variable name
choices.explanation: A structured list is a data abstraction. A library is a collection of pre-written procedures that programmers can use without writing those procedures themselves.

choices.text: A collection of pre-written procedures that programmers can call without implementing the code themselves
choices.explanation: A library provides reusable pre-written code (procedures, functions, APIs) that abstracts away implementation details — programmers call library procedures without needing to understand or rewrite them.

choices.text: A storage location that holds documentation about a program
choices.explanation: Documentation describes how code works. A library contains executable, callable code — not documentation.

choices.text: An error that occurs when a variable is used before it is assigned a value
choices.explanation: This describes an uninitialized variable error. A library is a collection of pre-written, reusable code.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.text: odd
choices.explanation: 4 MOD 2 = 0 (4 is evenly divisible by 2), so the condition is true and "even" is displayed.

choices.text: even
choices.explanation: MOD returns the remainder of division. 4 MOD 2 = 0, so the condition 0 = 0 is true, and the IF branch executes: DISPLAY("even").
0 is the result of 4 MOD 2, but that value is used only in the condition — it is not displayed. The IF branch displays "even".
The value of x (4) is used in the condition but is not displayed. The program displays either "even" or "odd" based on the condition result.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
myList ← [3, 7, 1, 9, 5]
REMOVE(myList, 2)
DISPLAY(myList[2])

questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: 7 was at index 2 before removal, but REMOVE(myList, 2) deletes the element at index 2 (value 7). After removal, the list is [3, 1, 9, 5], so myList[2] = 1.
REMOVE(myList, 2) removes the element at index 2, which is 7. The list becomes [3, 1, 9, 5]. After removal, myList[2] accesses the new second element: 1.
After removing index 2 (value 7), the list is [3, 1, 9, 5]. Index 2 now holds 1, not 9 (which is at index 3).
3 is at index 1 (the first element). After removing index 2, the list is [3, 1, 9, 5]. myList[2] = 1, not 3.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What values are displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: x increments by 2 each iteration, not by 1. The sequence is 1, 3, 5 — then x becomes 7 which exceeds 5, ending the loop.
x starts at 1 (not > 5, display 1, x becomes 3), then 3 (not > 5, display 3, x becomes 5), then 5 (not > 5, display 5, x becomes 7). 7 > 5 is true — loop ends. Displayed: 1, 3, 5.
The loop condition is checked before each iteration. When x = 7, 7 > 5 is true and the loop ends — 7 is never displayed.
x starts at 1 and is displayed before incrementing. The displayed values are the values of x at the top of each iteration: 1, 3, 5.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.text: true
choices.explanation: 9 MOD 2 = 1 (9 is odd). The condition 1 = 0 is false, so the ELSE branch executes and returns false.

choices.text: false
choices.explanation: isEven(9): 9 MOD 2 = 1 ≠ 0, so the condition is false. The ELSE branch returns false. DISPLAY(isEven(9)) outputs false.
1 is the result of 9 MOD 2, used in the condition but not returned or displayed. The procedure returns a boolean (true or false).
9 is the argument passed to the procedure. The procedure returns true or false based on whether the argument is even. Since 9 is odd, it returns and displays false.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
a ← true
b ← false
c ← NOT a OR b
DISPLAY(c)

questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.text: true
choices.explanation: NOT a = NOT true = false. false OR false = false. The result is false, not true.

choices.text: false
choices.explanation: NOT a = NOT true = false. Then false OR b = false OR false = false. c is assigned false, and DISPLAY(c) outputs false.

choices.text: true false
choices.explanation: Only c is displayed. c = NOT a OR b = NOT true OR false = false OR false = false.

choices.text: The code will not run because OR requires both operands to be the same type
choices.explanation: In CB pseudocode, OR operates on boolean values — both NOT a and b are boolean (false and false respectively). The code runs and displays false.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed? (Assume LENGTH returns the number of characters in a string.)

choices.text: apple
choices.explanation: "apple" has 5 characters — the condition requires LENGTH > 5 (strictly greater), so 5 is not > 5 and "apple" is not displayed.

choices.text: banana and cherry
choices.explanation: "apple" = 5 chars (not > 5), "banana" = 6 chars (> 5, displayed), "cherry" = 6 chars (> 5, displayed), "date" = 4 chars (not > 5). Output: banana then cherry.

choices.text: apple, banana, cherry, date
choices.explanation: Only items with more than 5 characters are displayed. "date" (4 chars) and "apple" (5 chars) do not meet the condition.

choices.text: cherry
choices.explanation: Both "banana" (6 chars) and "cherry" (6 chars) have more than 5 characters and are displayed. "cherry" alone is not the complete output.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: 3 is the first element, used to initialize max. As the loop progresses, max is updated whenever a larger value is found — the final max is 9.
7 is the second element and temporarily becomes max. But 9 (the fourth element) is larger, so max is updated to 9.
max starts at 3. Loop: 3 not > 3 (no change), 7 > 3 → max = 7, 2 not > 7, 9 > 7 → max = 9, 1 not > 9. RETURN(9). Displayed: 9.
1 is the last element in the list but the smallest. The findMax procedure returns the largest value, which is 9.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.text: no
choices.explanation: x = 2 > 1 is true, and y = 3 < 5 is also true. true AND true = true, so the IF branch executes and displays "yes".

choices.text: yes
choices.explanation: x > 1 evaluates to 2 > 1 = true. y < 5 evaluates to 3 < 5 = true. true AND true = true, so the IF branch executes: DISPLAY("yes").

choices.text: yes no
choices.explanation: An IF/ELSE executes only one branch. Since the condition is true, only "yes" is displayed.

choices.text: Nothing is displayed
choices.explanation: The condition is true (both sub-conditions are true), so the IF branch executes and displays "yes".

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.text: false
choices.explanation: The target value 6 is in the list at index 4. When i = 4, numbers[4] = 6 = target, so found is set to true. DISPLAY(found) outputs true.

choices.text: true
choices.explanation: The loop performs a linear search for 6 in the list. When i = 4, numbers[4] = 6 matches the target — found is set to true. The loop terminates and DISPLAY(found) outputs true.
4 is the index where 6 is found, but the code displays the boolean variable found, not the index. found is true, so true is displayed.
6 is the target value, not what is displayed. The code displays the boolean found, which becomes true when the target is located in the list.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes an undecidable problem?

choices.text: A problem that takes too many steps to solve in a reasonable amount of time
choices.explanation: A problem that is solvable but takes a very long time is called an unreasonable-time problem (e.g., NP-hard). An undecidable problem cannot be solved by any algorithm — not just slowly.

choices.text: A problem for which no algorithm can always correctly determine yes or no for all possible inputs
choices.explanation: An undecidable problem has no algorithm that correctly answers it for every possible input. The halting problem is the classic example — no program can always determine whether another program will halt.

choices.text: A problem with multiple correct answers depending on the input
choices.explanation: Having multiple valid outputs is a property of some algorithms, but that is not what 'undecidable' means. An undecidable problem has no algorithm that reliably produces any correct answer for all inputs.

choices.text: A problem that cannot be solved by a single programmer working alone
choices.explanation: An undecidable problem cannot be solved by any algorithm — regardless of how many programmers work on it. It is a theoretical property of certain problem classes, not a resource constraint.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: The list has three negative numbers: -3, -1, -2. The procedure counts all numbers less than 0, which is 3.
The procedure counts elements less than 0. Checking each: -3 (count = 1), 5 (skip), -1 (count = 2), 8 (skip), -2 (count = 3), 4 (skip). RETURN(3).
6 is the total number of elements in the list. The procedure counts only the negative elements, which is 3.
-6 would be the sum of the negative values (-3 + -1 + -2 = -6). The procedure counts (tallies) the negative numbers — it does not sum them.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: The loop runs 4 times, adding n (= 4) each iteration: 0 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 4 = 16. The result is 16, not 4.
8 = n * 2. The loop adds n to result n times, which is n * n = 4 * 4 = 16.
mystery(4): result starts at 0, loop runs 4 times, each adding n = 4. result = 0 + 4 + 4 + 4 + 4 = 16. This computes n^2.
24 would result from adding 4 six times (4 × 6 = 24). The loop runs exactly n = 4 times, giving 4 × 4 = 16.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
data ← [7, 2, 9, 4, 6, 1, 8]
target ← 5

The list data is sorted in ascending order before binary search is applied.

questions.question: When performing binary search on the sorted version of data to find target = 5, which element is examined FIRST?

choices.explanation: Binary search starts at the middle element, not the first. The sorted list is [1, 2, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9] with 7 elements — the middle index is 4, which holds value 6.
After sorting, the list is [1, 2, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9]. With 7 elements, the middle index is 4 (⌊7/2⌋ + 1 = 4 in 1-based indexing). The element at index 4 is 6 — the first element examined.
1 is the first element of the sorted list. Binary search examines the middle element first — not the first element.
4 is at index 3 in the sorted list [1, 2, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9]. The middle index (index 4) holds the value 6, which is examined first in binary search.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.text: both
choices.explanation: "both" requires x > 5 AND y > 5. x = 10 > 5 is true, but y = 4 > 5 is false. true AND false = false, so the ELSE branch executes.

choices.text: one
choices.explanation: x > 5 AND y > 5 = true AND false = false (ELSE executes). In the ELSE, x > 5 OR y > 5 = true OR false = true. DISPLAY("one") executes.

choices.text: neither
choices.explanation: "neither" requires both x ≤ 5 AND y ≤ 5. x = 10 > 5 is true, so x > 5 OR y > 5 is true, and "one" is displayed instead.

choices.text: both one
choices.explanation: Only one IF/ELSE branch executes. Since x > 5 AND y > 5 is false, the outer ELSE executes, which then displays "one".

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes data abstraction?

choices.text: Hiding the internal steps of a procedure so it can be called by name
choices.explanation: Hiding procedure implementation details is procedural abstraction. Data abstraction groups multiple related values under a single name (like a list).

choices.text: Representing multiple related values under a single variable name, such as a list, to manage complexity
choices.explanation: Data abstraction uses structures like lists to group related values under one name, reducing complexity — instead of managing 20 separate score variables, you use one list.

choices.text: Removing all variables from a program to reduce memory usage
choices.explanation: Removing variables would make a program unable to store data. Data abstraction adds a layer of organization, not removal of variables.

choices.text: Compressing data before storing it in a variable
choices.explanation: Compression reduces file size. Data abstraction is about managing complexity by grouping related values — not about compressing them.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: The procedure builds a reversed version of the list. Starting at index 3 (value 3), then 2 (value 2), then 1 (value 1), it returns [3, 2, 1].

reversed starts empty. i = 3: APPEND(reversed, lst[3] = 3) → [3]. i = 2: APPEND(reversed, lst[2] = 2) → [3, 2]. i = 1: APPEND(reversed, lst[1] = 1) → [3, 2, 1]. i = 0 < 1 → loop ends. RETURN([3, 2, 1]).
The procedure starts from the last index and works backward, producing [3, 2, 1] in that order — not [2, 1, 3].
The procedure returns the entire reversed list, not just the first element appended. The output is [3, 2, 1].

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question:
Select TWO answers.

Which of the following are examples of iteration in CB pseudocode?

choices.text: REPEAT 5 TIMES { ... }
choices.explanation: REPEAT n TIMES repeats a block of code a fixed number of times — this is a count-controlled iteration construct in CB pseudocode.
IF/ELSE is selection — it executes one of two branches based on a condition. It does not repeat; it runs once.

choices.text: FOR EACH item IN list { ... }
choices.explanation: FOR EACH iterates over every element in a list, executing the code block once for each element — this is a list-traversal iteration construct.

choices.text: PROCEDURE name(param) { RETURN(value) }
choices.explanation: A PROCEDURE definition declares a named block of code — it is not itself an iteration. Iteration involves repeating code, not defining a procedure.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: The procedure multiplies each element by the factor (3). The original list [2, 4, 6, 8] is transformed to [6, 12, 18, 24].
This would result from adding 3 to each element. The operation is multiplication by 3: [2×3, 4×3, 6×3, 8×3] = [6, 12, 18, 24].
The loop multiplies each element by factor = 3: 2×3=6, 4×3=12, 6×3=18, 8×3=24. The returned list is [6, 12, 18, 24].
60 is not the output. The procedure returns the modified list, not a sum. The displayed result is [6, 12, 18, 24].

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Are the two code segments equivalent?

choices.text: No, because Segment I checks x > 10 while Segment II checks x ≤ 10 — they use different conditions
choices.explanation: Using different but logically complementary conditions does not make segments non-equivalent. x > 10 and x ≤ 10 are mutually exclusive and exhaustive — the output mapping is identical for all values of x.

choices.text: Yes, because both segments display "big" when x > 10 and "small" when x ≤ 10
choices.explanation: Tracing both: if x > 10, Segment I displays "big" (IF branch) and Segment II displays "big" (ELSE branch). If x ≤ 10, Segment I displays "small" (ELSE) and Segment II displays "small" (IF). Output is identical for all x.

choices.text: No, because Segment II will display nothing when x = 10
choices.explanation: When x = 10, x ≤ 10 is true, so Segment II displays "small". Segment I: 10 > 10 is false, so it also displays "small". Both segments handle x = 10 identically.

choices.text: No, because equivalent code segments must use identical conditions
choices.explanation: Two segments can be equivalent even with different (but logically complementary) conditions, as long as they produce the same output for every possible input.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: mystery2 computes the factorial of n. mystery2(5) = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120, not 5.
25 = 5^2. The procedure computes factorial: 5! = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120.
mystery2 computes factorial recursively. mystery2(5) = 5 × mystery2(4) = 5 × 4 × mystery2(3) = 5 × 4 × 3 × mystery2(2) = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × mystery2(1) = 5 × 4 × 3 × 2 × 1 = 120.

choices.text: The procedure will run forever because there is no loop
choices.explanation: The procedure uses recursion with a base case (n ≤ 1 returns 1). It will terminate — each call reduces n by 1 until it reaches the base case.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed? (Assume integer division.)

choices.explanation: Step 1: mid = (1+10)/2 = 5, lst[5] = 5 < 7 → low = 6. Step 2: mid = (6+10)/2 = 8, lst[8] = 8 > 7 → high = 7. Step 3: mid = (6+7)/2 = 6, lst[6] = 6 < 7 → low = 7. Step 4: mid = (7+7)/2 = 7, lst[7] = 7 = target → high = -1. It takes 4 steps.
Tracing: Step 1: mid = 5, lst[5] = 5 < 7 → low = 6. Step 2: mid = 8, lst[8] = 8 > 7 → high = 7. Step 3: mid = 6, lst[6] = 6 < 7 → low = 7. Step 4: mid = 7, lst[7] = 7 = 7. Found after 4 steps, not 3.

Step 1: mid = 5, lst[5] = 5 < 7 → low = 6. Step 2: mid = 8, lst[8] = 8 > 7 → high = 7. Step 3: mid = 6, lst[6] = 6 < 7 → low = 7. Step 4: mid = 7, lst[7] = 7 = target → high = -1. Loop ends. steps = 4.
7 is the target value. Binary search finds it in 4 steps, not 7. Linear search would take 7 steps (checking positions 1 through 7).

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed? (Assume + concatenates strings.)

choices.text: aabbc
choices.explanation: The procedure removes duplicate adjacent pairs. "a" pairs with the next "a" (i jumps by 2), "b" pairs with next "b" (i jumps by 2), then "c" has no match and is appended. Result is "c".

choices.text: abc
choices.explanation: When a character matches the next, both are skipped (i += 2). "a" and "a" are both skipped, "b" and "b" are both skipped, only "c" (unmatched) is kept. Result is "c".

choices.text: c
choices.explanation: i=1: str[1]='a'=str[2]='a' → i jumps to 3. i=3: str[3]='b'=str[4]='b' → i jumps to 5. i=5: str[5]='c', str[6] would be out of range (treated as not equal) → result='c', i=6. Loop ends. RETURN('c').

choices.text: Nothing is displayed
choices.explanation: The 'c' at the end of the string has no matching adjacent character, so it is appended to result. The procedure returns and displays 'c'.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A programmer is designing an algorithm to find all prime numbers up to 1,000,000. The algorithm checks each number from 2 to 1,000,000, and for each candidate number n, it divides n by every integer from 2 to n - 1 to check for divisors.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes this algorithm in terms of efficiency?

choices.text: The algorithm is efficient because it uses a loop, which is faster than using conditional statements
choices.explanation: Using a loop vs. conditionals does not determine efficiency. The algorithm is inefficient because the number of operations grows much faster than the input size.

choices.text: The algorithm is efficient because it checks each number independently without storing results
choices.explanation: Not storing results is a missed optimization opportunity (like the Sieve of Eratosthenes), and the algorithm checking n * n divisor attempts per number is what makes it inefficient.

choices.text: The algorithm is unreasonably slow for large inputs because for each number n, it performs up to n - 2 division operations
choices.explanation: The algorithm's work grows quadratically with input. For n = 1,000,000, the inner loop runs up to nearly 1,000,000 times per number — producing roughly a trillion operations total, which takes an unreasonable amount of time.

choices.text: The algorithm is undecidable because there is no known method to find all primes
choices.explanation: Finding primes is a decidable problem — many algorithms solve it. The issue here is efficiency (slow for large inputs), not decidability.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question:
Select TWO answers.

Which of the following calls to Grade return "B"?

choices.text: Grade(85)
choices.explanation: 85 ≥ 90? No. 85 ≥ 80? Yes → RETURN("B"). Grade(85) returns "B".

choices.text: Grade(90)
choices.explanation: 90 ≥ 90 is true → RETURN("A"). Grade(90) returns "A", not "B".

choices.text: Grade(80)
choices.explanation: 80 ≥ 90? No. 80 ≥ 80? Yes → RETURN("B"). Grade(80) returns "B".

choices.text: Grade(70)
choices.explanation: 70 ≥ 90? No. 70 ≥ 80? No. 70 ≥ 70? Yes → RETURN("C"). Grade(70) returns "C", not "B".

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: addUnique(myList, 3): 3 is already in the list — no append. addUnique(myList, 4): 4 is not in the list — 4 is appended. List becomes [1, 2, 3, 4] with length 4.
addUnique(myList, 3): found=true (3 exists), no APPEND. addUnique(myList, 4): found=false (4 doesn't exist), APPEND(myList, 4). myList = [1, 2, 3, 4]. LENGTH = 4.
5 would require both values (3 and 4) to be added to the original 3-element list. But 3 is already in the list, so only 4 is added. LENGTH = 4.
The procedure adds, not removes. Starting with [1, 2, 3], adding 4 (unique) gives LENGTH = 4.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A simulation is used to model the spread of a virus in a city. The simulation assigns each simulated person a status of "susceptible," "infected," or "recovered." Each time step, an infected person has a 30% chance of infecting each susceptible neighbor. The simulation runs for 100 time steps and reports the percentage of the population that was infected.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST explains a purpose of using a simulation in this scenario?

choices.text: The simulation guarantees a precise prediction of how many people will get sick in a real outbreak
choices.explanation: Simulations are models based on assumptions and probabilities — they cannot guarantee precise real-world predictions. They reveal possible outcomes and trends, not certainties.

choices.text: The simulation allows researchers to test different infection rates and intervention strategies without real-world risk
choices.explanation: Simulations model scenarios computationally, enabling researchers to test variables (like vaccination rates or social distancing) that would be unethical, expensive, or impossible to test on real populations.

choices.text: The simulation eliminates all uncertainty by using random probability values in each step
choices.explanation: Randomness in simulations introduces variation — it models uncertainty rather than eliminating it. Running the simulation multiple times with the same parameters will produce different outcomes.

choices.text: The simulation is used as a library that other programs can call to solve virus spread problems
choices.explanation: A library is a collection of reusable procedures. A simulation is a model that runs computational experiments — it may use libraries but is not itself a library.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the relationship between segmentA and segmentB?

choices.text: segmentA is more efficient because it uses iteration rather than recursion
choices.explanation: Both procedures perform the same number of multiplications for input n. The key relationship is equivalence — they produce the same output for all valid inputs. Efficiency difference between the two is minor in this case.

choices.text: The two procedures produce different results for the same input
choices.explanation: Both compute 2^n. segmentA multiplies result by 2 exactly n times starting from 1. segmentB recursively doubles 1 n times. They produce identical results.

choices.text: The two procedures are equivalent — both compute 2^n for any non-negative integer n
choices.explanation: segmentA: starts at 1, doubles n times → 2^n. segmentB: base case 2^0 = 1, recursive step 2 × 2^(n-1) = 2^n. Both return 2^n — they are equivalent algorithms.

choices.text: segmentB is undecidable because it calls itself
choices.explanation: Recursive procedures are perfectly valid and decidable when they have a base case that terminates the recursion — segmentB has the base case n = 0.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: 22 = 12 + 10. But 8 also meets both conditions (8 > 6 AND 8 MOD 2 = 0). The correct total is 12 + 8 + 10 = 30.
35 would include odd numbers. The condition requires val > 6 AND val MOD 2 = 0 (even). Only 12, 8, and 10 meet both conditions: 12 + 8 + 10 = 30.
62 would be the sum of all elements (5+12+3+8+15+7+2+10=62). The conditions filter only values > 6 AND even: 12, 8, 10. Total = 30.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question:
Select TWO answers.

A programmer has a list of 1,000,000 elements that she knows is sorted in ascending order. She needs to determine whether a specific value exists in the list. Which of the following statements about algorithm choice are correct?

choices.text: Binary search would find the answer in at most about 20 steps because it halves the search space each step
choices.explanation: log₂(1,000,000) ≈ 20. Binary search halves the search space with each comparison — on 1 million elements it takes at most about 20 comparisons.

choices.text: Linear search would always be faster than binary search for sorted lists
choices.explanation: Linear search could require checking all 1,000,000 elements. Binary search requires at most ~20 comparisons. Binary search is dramatically faster on large sorted lists.

choices.text: Binary search requires the list to be sorted in order to work correctly
choices.explanation: Binary search works by comparing the target to the middle element and deciding whether to search the left or right half — this logic only works if the list is sorted.

choices.text: Linear search cannot be used on a sorted list
choices.explanation: Linear search works on both sorted and unsorted lists — it checks each element sequentially. It is simply less efficient than binary search on sorted lists.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: These are the even numbers up to 8. The condition i MOD 2 ≠ 0 selects odd numbers (those that have a non-zero remainder when divided by 2).
i goes from 1 to 8. i MOD 2 ≠ 0 is true for odd numbers: 1, 3, 5, 7 are appended. 8 is even, so 8 MOD 2 = 0 and 8 is not appended. Result: [1, 3, 5, 7].
The loop stops when i > 8. 9 > 8 is true when i reaches 9, so 9 is never checked or appended. The result is [1, 3, 5, 7].
The condition filters out even numbers. Only values where i MOD 2 ≠ 0 (odd numbers) are appended: [1, 3, 5, 7].

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content: A programmer writes an API (application programming interface) that allows other programmers to call a procedure named sortList. The sortList procedure takes a list as input and returns the list sorted in ascending order. The documentation says: 'Takes a list, returns sorted list.' The internal implementation uses a specific sorting algorithm that the caller does not need to know.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST explains how sortList demonstrates procedural abstraction?

choices.text: It demonstrates procedural abstraction because it is written in CB pseudocode, which is abstract
choices.explanation: The choice of programming language or pseudocode is not what makes something procedural abstraction. Abstraction is about hiding implementation details behind a named interface.

choices.text: It demonstrates procedural abstraction by allowing callers to use the sorting functionality without knowing the internal algorithm
choices.explanation: Procedural abstraction hides implementation details — callers use sortList by providing a list and receiving a sorted result, without needing to understand the sorting algorithm used internally.

choices.text: It demonstrates procedural abstraction because the procedure sorts data, which is an abstract concept
choices.explanation: Abstraction refers to hiding implementation details, not to whether the concept being computed (sorting) is abstract. Procedural abstraction means the caller does not need to know how the sorting is done.

choices.text: It does not demonstrate procedural abstraction because the caller must know what sorting algorithm is used
choices.explanation: The scenario explicitly states the caller does not need to know the internal implementation. This is the defining characteristic of procedural abstraction.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: flatten takes a 2D list (list of lists) and returns a single flat list. The nested loops iterate over each row, then each value, appending all values to result.
FOR EACH row IN grid: row=[1,2] → APPEND 1, APPEND 2; row=[3,4] → APPEND 3, APPEND 4; row=[5,6] → APPEND 5, APPEND 6. result = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6].
This would be only the first elements of each row. The inner loop iterates over every value in each row, appending all of them.
6 is the number of elements or the last element. The procedure returns the full flattened list [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6].

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following problems is an example of an undecidable problem?

choices.text: Determining whether a number is prime
choices.explanation: Primality testing is decidable — algorithms exist (like trial division or the Miller-Rabin test) that always correctly answer whether a number is prime.

choices.text: Finding the largest element in a list of numbers
choices.explanation: Finding the maximum element is decidable — a simple linear scan always finds the largest element in a finite list.

choices.text: Determining, for any program and any input, whether the program will eventually stop or run forever
choices.explanation: This is the Halting Problem — the classic undecidable problem. Alan Turing proved no algorithm can always correctly determine whether an arbitrary program halts or runs forever on a given input.

choices.text: Sorting a list of numbers in ascending order
choices.explanation: Sorting is decidable — many algorithms (bubble sort, merge sort, quicksort) always correctly sort a finite list.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: This would be a right rotation. rotateLeft moves the first element to the end: [10, 20, 30, 40] → remove 10 → [20, 30, 40] → append 10 → [20, 30, 40, 10].
first = lst[1] = 10. REMOVE(lst, 1) → lst = [20, 30, 40]. APPEND(lst, first = 10) → lst = [20, 30, 40, 10]. RETURN([20, 30, 40, 10]).
This is the original list unchanged. The procedure removes the first element and appends it to the end, producing [20, 30, 40, 10].
This would require two left rotations. A single rotateLeft moves only the first element to the end: [10, 20, 30, 40] → [20, 30, 40, 10].

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes what isBalanced appears to determine about lst?

choices.text: Whether the list is sorted in ascending order
choices.explanation: The procedure doesn't compare adjacent elements for ordering. It checks whether any element's value equals its own index — a 'fixed point' search.

choices.text: Whether any element in the list has a value equal to its own index position
choices.explanation: The procedure uses a binary search-like approach to find a 'fixed point' — a position mid where lst[mid] = mid. It returns true if any such position exists.

choices.text: Whether all elements in the list are positive numbers
choices.explanation: The procedure does not check for positivity of values. It checks whether an element equals its index (a fixed point), which is a specific mathematical property.

choices.text: Whether the list contains duplicate values
choices.explanation: The procedure never compares elements to each other. It compares each element's value to its index position — checking for fixed points, not duplicates.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A programmer needs to determine whether two code segments produce identical outputs for all possible inputs. Which of the following BEST describes the approach she should take?

choices.text: Run both segments with one test case — if the outputs match, the segments are equivalent
choices.explanation: One matching test case does not prove equivalence for all inputs. Two segments could produce identical output for some inputs but different outputs for others.

choices.text: Trace through the logic of both segments for all possible input cases to verify the output is the same in each case
choices.explanation: Proving equivalence requires verifying that both segments produce identical outputs for every possible input — either through exhaustive testing (for finite inputs) or logical reasoning about the code's behavior.

choices.text: Count the number of lines in each segment — equivalent segments always have the same number of lines
choices.explanation: Line count has no relation to equivalence. Two programs can produce identical behavior with very different numbers of lines of code.

choices.text: Check whether both segments use the same variables — equivalent segments always use the same variable names
choices.explanation: Variable names are arbitrary identifiers. Two equivalent segments can use completely different variable names and still produce the same output for all inputs.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: What is displayed when the code segment is executed?

choices.explanation: 3 would be the count of negative values. The procedure keeps positive values (x > 0): 5, 8, 7 — that is 3, but 0 is not included. Wait: 5 (positive), 8 (positive), 7 (positive) = 3 positive values. But the question is: is 0 counted? 0 > 0 is false, so 0 is excluded. There are exactly 3 positive numbers: 5, 8, 7.
Correct — 5, 8, and 7 are the only elements where x > 0 is true. LENGTH = 3.
Checking each element: -3 (skip), 5 (keep), -1 (skip), 0 (skip, 0 is not > 0), 8 (keep), -2 (skip), 7 (keep). result = [5, 8, 7]. LENGTH = 3.
4 would include 0 in the result. The condition x > 0 is strictly greater than 0 — 0 itself is excluded. Only 5, 8, and 7 are positive: LENGTH = 3.

questions.subject: ap-csp

stimulus.content:
A programmer writes a procedure to check whether a password meets security requirements:
1. At least 8 characters long
2. Contains at least one uppercase letter
3. Contains at least one digit

The pseudocode uses helper procedures hasUpper(s) and hasDigit(s) that return true/false.

questions.question: Which of the following pseudocode segments correctly implements all three password requirements?

choices.text: IF(LENGTH(pw) ≥ 8 AND hasUpper(pw) AND hasDigit(pw)) { RETURN(true) } ELSE { RETURN(false) }
choices.explanation: This correctly checks all three conditions using AND — the password must meet all three requirements simultaneously: at least 8 characters, has an uppercase letter, and has a digit.

choices.text: IF(LENGTH(pw) ≥ 8 OR hasUpper(pw) OR hasDigit(pw)) { RETURN(true) } ELSE { RETURN(false) }
choices.explanation: Using OR means the password passes if it meets ANY one condition. This allows a 1-character password with no uppercase or digit to pass — all three conditions must be satisfied simultaneously (AND).

choices.text: IF(LENGTH(pw) ≥ 8) { IF(hasUpper(pw)) { RETURN(true) } ELSE { RETURN(false) } }
choices.explanation: This only checks two conditions (length and uppercase) — it never checks for a digit. All three requirements must be verified.

choices.text: IF(NOT LENGTH(pw) < 8 AND NOT hasUpper(pw) AND NOT hasDigit(pw)) { RETURN(true) }
choices.explanation: NOT hasUpper(pw) means 'does NOT have uppercase' — this inverts the second and third conditions. This segment returns true when the password lacks uppercase and lacks a digit, which is the opposite of what is required.

questions.subject: ap-csp
questions.question: A student claims sumEvens and sumEvensV2 are equivalent. For which input would they produce DIFFERENT outputs?

choices.explanation: All elements are even, so sumEvens adds all of them and sumEvensV2 also adds all of them (all pass the even check). Both produce 20 — no difference.
sumEvens adds all elements: 1+3+5+7 = 16. sumEvensV2 only adds even elements: none of 1, 3, 5, 7 are even → total = 0. Different outputs (16 vs 0).
For an empty list, both procedures start with total = 0 and the loop never executes. Both return 0 — same output.
All elements (0, 2, 4) are even, so sumEvensV2's condition passes for all. sumEvens and sumEvensV2 both produce 6 — same output.
